title: My Orcha'd In Linden Lea
content:
ï¿½Ithin the woodlands, flowï¿½ry gleaded,
By the woak treeï¿½s mossy moot,
The sheenen grass-bleades, timber-sheaded,
Now do quiver under voot;
Anï¿½ birds do whissle over head,
Anï¿½ waterï¿½s bubblen in its bed,
Anï¿½ there vor me the apple tree
Do lean down low in Linden Lea.
When leaves that leately wer a-springen
Now do feade ï¿½ithin the copse,
Anï¿½ painted birds do hush their zingen
Up upon the timberï¿½s tops;
Anï¿½ brown-leavï¿½d fruitï¿½s a-turnen red,
In cloudless zunsheen, over head,
Wiï¿½ fruit vor me, the apple tree
Do lean down low in Linden Lea.

Let other voï¿½k meake money vaster
In the air oï¿½ dark-roomï¿½d towns,
I donï¿½t dread a peevish measter;
Though noo man do heed my frowns,
I be free to goo abrode,
Or teake agean my hwomeward road
To where, vor me, the apple tree
Do lean down low in Linden Lea.
meaning:
William Barnes's contribution to classic is further solidified by the brilliance found in "My Orcha'd In Linden Lea"...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
